content.text: Every call to a language model is constrained by a

— a hard ceiling on how many tokens the model can attend to at once. Understanding how text becomes tokens, how those tokens fill the window, and how to measure and manage the budget precisely is foundational to building reliable agents, RAG pipelines, and long-running LLM systems.

is the atomic unit a language model processes. Rather than operating on raw characters or Unicode code points, modern LLMs use subword tokenizers (BPE, WordPiece, or Unigram LM) that split text into variable-length chunks — common words become single tokens, rarer words split into several pieces. Each chunk is assigned a unique integer ID. The model never sees your text as letters; it sees a sequence of these integer IDs.

is the maximum length of that ID sequence in a single forward pass. Window sizes are published by providers: GPT-4o supports 128K tokens, Claude 3 Opus supports 200K, Gemini 1.5 Pro up to 1M. Exceeding this limit causes an API error (or silent truncation in some implementations). Tokens from the prompt and tokens generated in the response both count against the same budget on most APIs. The window is not a byte limit, a word limit, or a character limit — it is exclusively a
after the model's specific tokenizer has encoded the text.

Different tokenizers produce different counts for the same text. English prose averages roughly 4 characters per token with cl100k_base (OpenAI), but Python source code, JSON, URLs, and non-English scripts can be considerably more expensive. A sentence that encodes to 50 tokens in one tokenizer may produce 65 tokens in another — a 30% difference that compounds quickly across a large context.

Agents accumulate context across turns: a system prompt, tool definitions, the evolving conversation history, retrieved document chunks, and any intermediate reasoning. Each component consumes tokens. An agent that starts with a 3K-token system prompt and 12K of tool schemas has already consumed 15K tokens before the first user message. Across a 30-turn conversation, even 200-token-per-turn additions fill a 128K window in under 200 turns — but with RAG injections of 50K tokens per query, that same window can fill after a single turn.

Under-counting leads to silent truncation — the API silently drops the oldest history or the lowest-priority RAG content, causing the model to answer without key context. Over-counting wastes tokens, increases latency, and raises cost. Token-accurate budgeting is the prerequisite to every context management strategy: sliding windows, conversation summarization, and relevance-ranked RAG all depend on knowing exactly how many tokens each component consumes.

Cost is equally direct: LLM providers charge per token. A system that overestimates token usage by 20% and consequently sends 20% more content per request will pay 20% more. At production scale — millions of requests per day — that difference is material.
— encode-before-send gives exact remaining headroom versus the provider cap, eliminating guesswork from context planning.
— since pricing is per million tokens, measuring input tokens before the request allows precise cost estimates.
— measure fixed overhead (system prompt + tool schemas) once per session, then fill only the remaining budget with retrieved chunks.
— context-exceeded errors map to measured token counts; pinpointing which component overfills the window is straightforward.
— agents that know their exact token usage can make consistent decisions about whether to summarize history, drop old turns, or route to a higher-capacity model.
Prefill latency scales with token count — very long contexts increase time-to-first-token, sometimes dramatically, regardless of how much of that context is actually relevant.
Larger windows cost more — many providers price input and output tokens separately; a 128K-token call at $10/MTok costs $1.28 in input alone, before any output.

Tokenizer differences complicate model migration — switching from GPT-4o to Claude or an open-weight model changes token counts for the same content; budget logic tied to one tokenizer must be recalibrated.
Chat template overhead is invisible in raw text — role markers, special tokens (<|im_start|>, <|endoftext|>), and per-turn delimiters add tokens not present in the message strings.
Max output reservation reduces effective input — on combined-limit APIs, reserving 4K tokens for output leaves only max_context − 4K for input even if you pass max_tokens=4096.

Raw text passes through the tokenizer's encoder, which first splits it into characters (or bytes), then merges adjacent pairs according to a learned merge table (BPE) or selects from a probability-weighted vocabulary (Unigram LM). The result is a list of integer IDs — the
. Its length is the token count. This count is exact and reproducible; the same text always produces the same token count for the same tokenizer version.

In a chat-format API call, the full token count includes: the system message, all user and assistant turns, any tool/function definitions (passed as JSON schemas), tool call results injected as user turns, retrieved documents or RAG content, and any special tokens added by the chat template (role delimiters, BOS/EOS markers). Providers add these wrappers automatically at the API layer; client-side token counting must apply the same chat template to get an accurate number.
On a combined-limit API, the constraint is:
Rearranging to find the available input headroom before adding RAG content:
includes the system prompt, tool schemas, and the accumulated conversation history. This value must be recomputed each turn because history grows.

Content type significantly affects token density. English prose encodes at roughly 1 token per 4 characters. Dense Python source code can be 1 token per 2.5 characters. JSON-formatted tool results, which repeat key names and brackets, may cost 1 token per 2 characters. Non-ASCII languages can cost 2–5 tokens per character for scripts not well-represented in the training corpus. Understanding these ratios helps estimate whether a document will fit before committing to an encode call.
The diagram below shows both how the context window budget is divided among its components and the token counting pipeline that feeds the budget check before each API call.
Token-level context measurement is required in any LLM application that does not send trivially short prompts. Specifically:
— measure fixed overhead (system prompt + tools) once per session; fill remaining budget with the top-ranked chunks.
— re-count after every turn; trigger history pruning or summarization when the accumulated history exceeds a threshold.
— compare effective input headroom across candidate models after subtracting tool schema size and reserved output.
— multiply token counts by per-million-token pricing before committing to expensive API calls.
— pre-segment documents into chunks that fit the window before submitting.

Token counting is not necessary for quick prototypes that always send short prompts or for toy scripts where context overflow is not a practical concern. In all production settings it should be considered standard practice.
is used by GPT-4, GPT-3.5-turbo, and text-embedding-ada-002.

is used by GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini. For open-weight models (LLaMA, Mistral, Gemma), load the tokenizer from the model's Hugging Face repository. Anthropic's API returns token counts in the response metadata rather than providing a client-side tokenizer, though tiktoken's cl100k_base is a reasonable approximation for Claude models.
Step 2: Count the full formatted prompt
For OpenAI-style chat APIs, each message contributes not only its content but also tokens for the role name, separator tokens, and the generation prompt. The canonical formula is:

The constant 4 accounts for per-message overhead tokens; the trailing 2 accounts for the generation prompt. The exact values depend on the model version and can be verified against the provider's documentation.
Step 4: Apply chat templates for open-weight models
For HuggingFace-hosted models, the tokenizer carries the model's chat template. Applying it before counting produces the exact token sequence the model will see, including all special tokens.

"""hf_token_count.py — count tokens using a HuggingFace tokenizer with chat template."""
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

def count_chat_tokens(model_id: str, messages: list[dict]) -> int:
    """
    Count the number of tokens that the model will actually receive,
    including role markers and special tokens added by the chat template.
    """
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
    # apply_chat_template formats messages exactly as the model expects
    formatted = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
        messages,
        tokenize=False,
        add_generation_prompt=True,
    )
    return len(tokenizer.encode(formatted))


def fit_rag_chunks(
    model_id: str,
    system: str,
    history: list[dict],
    chunks: list[str],
    max_context: int,
    reserved_output: int = 2048,
) -> list[str]:
    """
    Greedily select RAG chunks that fit in the remaining token budget.
    Returns selected chunks in the order provided.
    """
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)
    base_messages = [{"role": "system", "content": system}] + history
    base_tokens = count_chat_tokens(model_id, base_messages)  # type: ignore[arg-type]
    available = max_context - reserved_output - base_tokens

    selected: list[str] = []
    used = 0
    for chunk in chunks:
        chunk_tokens = len(tokenizer.encode(chunk))
        if used + chunk_tokens > available:
            break
        selected.append(chunk)
        used += chunk_tokens

    return selected


if __name__ == "__main__":
    model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct"
    messages = [
        {"role": "system", "content": "Answer concisely."},
        {"role": "user",   "content": "What is BPE tokenization?"},
    ]
    n = count_chat_tokens(model_id, messages)
    print(f"Token count (with chat template): {n}")
Simple: measuring token counts by content type

import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base")

samples = {
    "English prose": "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. " * 20,
    "Python source": "def factorial(n):\n    return 1 if n <= 1 else n * factorial(n - 1)\n" * 10,
    "JSON payload": '{"key": "value", "count": 42, "active": true}\n' * 20,
    "SQL query":    "SELECT id, name, created_at FROM users WHERE active = TRUE ORDER BY created_at DESC;\n" * 10,
}

for label, text in samples.items():
    chars = len(text)
    tokens = len(enc.encode(text))
    ratio = chars / tokens
    print(f"{label:20s}  chars={chars:5d}  tokens={tokens:5d}  chars/token={ratio:.2f}")

# Expected output (approximate):
# English prose        chars=  900  tokens=  210  chars/token=4.29
# Python source        chars=  720  tokens=  210  chars/token=3.43
# JSON payload         chars=  800  tokens=  280  chars/token=2.86
# SQL query            chars=  880  tokens=  200  chars/token=4.40

The output illustrates why content type matters: JSON is 50% more token-expensive per character than English prose. An agent that ingests large JSON tool responses will consume the context window much faster than one working with natural language.

A legal document analysis agent uses GPT-4o (128K context) to answer questions about uploaded contracts. Each call includes: a 2K-token system prompt, a 15K-token tool schema defining 8 tools (search, extract, compare, summarize, validate, flag, annotate, cite), and a 5K-token conversation history. Fixed overhead is 22K tokens, leaving 106K for the contract and the reserved output.

A 200-page contract encodes to roughly 150K tokens — too large to fit in one call. The team implements a segment-and-query strategy: the contract is split into 10K-token chunks (each processed with a preceding 1K-token overlap), stored in a vector database, and retrieved by relevance. At query time, the top 3–5 chunks are injected into the remaining
tokens of input headroom. The pre-flight encode step runs in under 10ms on a modern CPU and prevents the otherwise-silent truncation that caused wrong answers in the original prototype.
is unreliable across content types and non-English text. Always encode with the model's actual tokenizer.

— a function-calling agent passes JSON schemas for every available tool in every request. Ten tools with detailed parameter descriptions can easily consume 8–15K tokens, a cost often overlooked during capacity planning.
Counting only message content, not chat template
— adding up the character counts of message strings without applying the chat template understates the true token count by 3–5% on typical prompts.
reserves 4096 output tokens from the total. The effective input limit is
— token usage grows with each turn. An agent that counts only at initialization will silently exceed the budget as conversation history accumulates.
Using the wrong tokenizer for the deployed model
— cl100k_base token counts are incorrect for models using o200k_base (GPT-4o family), which adds tokens for new multi-lingual and code characters. Errors of 5–10% accumulate.
— compute token count for the fully assembled prompt before sending. Reject or prune before hitting the API to avoid error-response latency.
— the system prompt and tool schemas rarely change within a session. Encode them once at startup and store the count; do not re-encode on every turn.
— establish a minimum output reservation and subtract it from max_context before sizing any variable content.
— provider responses include actual input and output token counts. Log them and compare to pre-flight estimates; significant divergence indicates a tokenizer mismatch.
Prune by relevance, not by age alone
— when dropping history to fit the budget, prefer removing low-relevance turns rather than always removing the oldest; the earliest system-level context may be the most critical.
— target 90–95% of the effective input budget rather than the absolute maximum; small differences between client-side estimates and server-side counts can push a near-limit request over the edge.

Token encoding throughput with tiktoken is typically 1–3 million tokens per second on a single CPU core. Encoding a 128K-token prompt takes under 100ms — negligible compared to the API latency of 5–30 seconds for the same call. There is no practical reason to skip pre-flight encoding on performance grounds.
The more significant performance consideration is

at the server: the transformer must attend over every input token before generating the first output token. Prefill latency scales roughly linearly with input token count at moderate window sizes but grows super-linearly for very long contexts on non-sparse-attention models. A 128K-token prompt may have a time-to-first-token (TTFT) 10–50x higher than a 4K prompt on the same model, depending on the provider's hardware and batching strategy.

Prompt caching addresses prefill cost for repeated static prefixes. Providers including Anthropic and OpenAI offer caching where the KV cache of a frequently repeated system prompt or document block is reused across requests. Only the changed suffix needs re-prefilling. Effective use requires structuring prompts so the static content appears first and the variable content appears last.
tiktoken is OpenAI's fast BPE tokenizer library, implemented in Rust with Python bindings. Install with
. It supports direct model name lookup (
). It is the authoritative client-side tokenizer for all OpenAI and Azure OpenAI models.
, which loads the tokenizer corresponding to any model on the HuggingFace Hub and includes the
method for handling role markers and special tokens. The
library (also by HuggingFace) provides the underlying Rust implementation for high-throughput batch encoding.

for a provider-agnostic interface. It abstracts over tiktoken (for OpenAI models), HuggingFace tokenizers (for open-weight models), and provider-specific token counting APIs, making it useful in multi-model agent frameworks where the same budget logic must work across providers.
LangChain integrates token counting through its callback system.
tracks cumulative token usage across chains and agents. The
methods delegate to the underlying tokenizer, providing a consistent interface across LLM backends.
Several approaches exist for managing context limits. The table below compares them on accuracy, implementation effort, and suitability for production use.
Silent truncation is a data integrity risk

Some API clients or middleware layers silently truncate prompts that exceed the context limit rather than raising an error. The model then receives an incomplete prompt — often missing the end of the instruction or the middle of a document — and generates a plausible-sounding but incorrect answer with no indication that data was dropped. In legal, medical, or financial contexts, this is a reliability failure that can have real consequences.
Always validate the pre-flight token count against the hard limit and fail explicitly (
or return an error response) rather than allowing the application layer to proceed with a truncated prompt.

Attackers can craft inputs — document uploads, user messages, tool call results — that are deliberately oversized, causing the system prompt or safety instructions at the beginning of the context to fall out of the effective attention window (a concern with very long contexts and known "lost in the middle" attention degradation). Defense includes capping user-supplied content to an explicit token budget, placing critical safety instructions near the end of the prompt as well as the beginning, and verifying that system prompt content is actually attended to on long-context requests.

An agent loop with no context pruning can grow its history to the maximum context window length in as few as 10–20 turns on a long-context model, causing each subsequent API call to cost 10–20x more than expected. Budget anomaly detection — alerting when a single request exceeds a token threshold — prevents cost spikes from going unnoticed. Set hard token limits in orchestration code, not just in the API
Picture the context window as a
fixed-length shelf measured in token IDs

. Every prompt component and every generated token occupies shelf space. The shelf has no concept of words, sentences, or paragraphs — only counts of subword pieces. Longer words and unusual characters occupy more pieces than common short words. When the shelf is full, nothing more can be placed on it until something is removed. The system prompt at one end and the reserved output space at the other end define immovable bookends; everything else — history, RAG, tool results — competes for the space between them.

A practical corollary: measuring the shelf before each call is equivalent to measuring a truck's load before driving under a bridge. The measurement takes seconds; the alternative — learning the limit the hard way — is far more costly.
Context windows are measured in token IDs produced by the model's specific tokenizer — not characters, words, or bytes.
On most combined-limit APIs, output tokens are subtracted from the same budget as input tokens; effective input headroom is max_context minus reserved output.
Content type determines token density: JSON and source code are 30–50% more expensive per character than English prose.
Fixed overhead — system prompt, tool schemas, and accumulated history — must be measured with the chat template applied; tool schemas alone can consume 5–15K tokens.
Pre-flight encoding with tiktoken or the HuggingFace tokenizer prevents silent truncation, context-exceeded errors, and cost anomalies.
Cache token counts for static content (system prompt, tool schemas) and re-count per-turn content (history, RAG) on every orchestration iteration.
Prompt caching at the API layer amortizes prefill cost for frequently repeated static prefixes; structure prompts with stable content first and variable content last.
Treat context overflow as a data integrity risk: silent truncation produces plausible but wrong answers with no error signal.
